title: Sanskrit Grammar Evolution
summary: The community revolves around the ancient grammar of Sanskrit, with "Ashtadhyayi" being a key text that describes this foundational linguistic work.
findings: Ashtadhyayi is explicitly described as an ancient grammar of Sanskrit.
Sanskrit uses and relies on the concepts presented in the ancient grammar documented by Ashtadhyayi.

nodes: derivational morphemes such as un- and -ness create new words (happy -> unhappiness)
title: Word Derivational Morphemes
summary: This community focuses on how derivational morphemes like un- and -ness are used to create new words, exemplified by the transformation of "happy" into "unhappiness."
findings: The relation between "-ness" and "derivational morphemes such as un-" demonstrates their role in word creation.
Un- is identified as an example of a derivational prefix that changes the meaning of words.

title: Bloomfield's Linguistic Work
summary: Leonard Bloomfield, a linguist, wrote "Language" in 1933, which is the subject of this community.
findings: Leonard Bloomfield authored the book "Language."
The publication "Language" was written by Bloomfield in 1933.

title: Linguistic Foundations
summary: The community of entities including "Morphology" and "word structure" is fundamentally linked, with Morphology providing the foundational rules that govern how words are structured.
findings: Morphology directly influences word structure by defining grammatical rules.
This relationship illustrates a hierarchical dependency where higher-level linguistic concepts guide lower-level components.
The interplay between these entities exemplifies the systematic nature of language construction.

title: Language Morpheme Core
summary: This community connects the concept of a morpheme, which is a fundamental building block in language, to its smallest meaningful unit role.
findings: A morpheme is explicitly defined as the smallest meaningful unit of language.
This relationship clarifies the foundational structure of linguistic elements.

nodes: bound morphemes like the plural suffix -s must attach to a stem
title: Suffix Attachment Rules
summary: This community centers around the rules governing how plural suffix -s attaches to bound morphemes, forming a stem.
findings: The plural suffix -s depends on bound morphemes like it must attach to a stem.
These entities clarify that the suffix is not an independent unit but part of a larger linguistic structure.
The community highlights the necessity and conditions under which this attachment occurs in grammatical processes.

nodes: inflectional morphemes like -ed and -ing mark grammar without changing word class
title: English Grammar Morphology
summary: The community of entities includes the English language and inflectional morphemes, which are crucial for grammatical functions without altering word class.
findings: Inflectional morphemes like -ed and -ing are essential for English grammar.
These morphemes modify words to indicate tense or state but do not change their fundamental role in sentences.
The relationship between English and these inflectional morphemes highlights the importance of grammatical markers in language structure.

title: Emotional Dynamics Community
summary: This community consists of the entities "happy" and "unhappiness," where "happy" is a state that can be derived from or transformed into "unhappiness."
findings: Happiness and unhappiness are opposites, with happiness being the absence of unhappiness.
The relation "derives_from" indicates that conditions leading to happiness can also lead to the emergence of unhappiness.

nodes: free morphemes like book stand alone
title: Word Structure Community
summary: This community consists of books as examples of free morphemes, which are standalone words that can stand alone without a prefix or suffix.
findings: Books exemplify the concept of free morphemes.
Free morphemes like "book" do not require additional affixes to function independently.

title: Word Derivation Community
summary: The community connects words with their derivational morphemes, illustrating how specific suffixes like "-ness" transform the root word "happy" into a new concept of unhappiness.
findings: Derivational morphemes are crucial for forming new words from existing ones.
The relation between "happy" and "unhappiness" demonstrates the transformation process through a derivational morpheme "-ness".
This community highlights how linguistic rules guide word creation in natural language processing tasks.

nodes: mark grammar without changing word class
title: Morpheme-Verb Relationship
summary: This community connects inflectional morphemes to "mark grammar without changing word class," illustrating how these morphemes function in language structure.
findings: Inflectional morphemes are defined as elements that modify words while maintaining their original part of speech.
The relation shows that certain morphemes serve the purpose of marking grammatical features without altering the basic word class.

title: The TRI family — three
summary: Meet Tri, a tiny magical friend. The root TRI means 'three'.
findings: triangle: a shape with three corners
tricycle: a cycle with three wheels
trio: three friends together

title: The UNI family — one
summary: Meet Uni, who loves being the only one. The root UNI means 'one'.
findings: unicorn: a horse with one horn
unicycle: a cycle with one wheel
uniform: one same dress for everyone

title: The BI family — two
summary: Meet Bi, who always comes in twos. The root BI means 'two'.
findings: bicycle: a cycle with two wheels
binoculars: two tubes to see far
biped: an animal with two feet

title: The OCTO family — eight
summary: Meet Octo, the friend with eight of everything. The root OCTO means 'eight'.
findings: octopus: a sea animal with eight arms
octagon: a shape with eight sides
october: once the eighth month

title: The CENT family — hundred
summary: Meet Cent, who loves the big number hundred! The root CENT means 'hundred'.
findings: century: a hundred years
centipede: a bug with many (a hundred!) feet
percent: out of a hundred

title: The DENT family — tooth
summary: Meet Denta, who keeps every tooth shiny. The root DENT means 'tooth'.
findings: dentist: a doctor for teeth
dental: about teeth
trident: a spear with three teeth

title: The PED family — foot
summary: Meet Ped, who is all about feet! The root PED means 'foot'.
findings: pedal: a lever you push with your foot
pedestrian: a person going on foot
tripod: a stand with three feet

title: The MANU family — hand
summary: Meet Manny, whose hands can make anything. The root MANU means 'hand'.
findings: manual: done by hand
manicure: care for the hands
manufacture: to make (first, by hand)

title: The AQUA family — water
summary: Meet Aqua, who carries water everywhere. The root AQUA means 'water'.
findings: aquarium: a glass home for fish and water
aquatic: living in water
aqueduct: a bridge that carries water

title: The SOL family — sun
summary: Meet Sol, bright and warm like the sun. The root SOL means 'sun'.
findings: solar: about the sun
solarium: a sunny glass room
parasol: a shade against the sun

title: The LUNA family — moon
summary: Meet Luna, soft and silver like the moon. The root LUNA means 'moon'.
findings: lunar: about the moon
lunatic: once meant 'moon-struck'
lunamoth: a moth pale as the moon

title: The TERR family — earth, land
summary: Meet Terra, who loves the good brown earth. The root TERR means 'earth, land'.
findings: terrain: the shape of the land
territory: an area of land
terrarium: a little glass garden of earth

title: The FLOR family — flower
summary: Meet Flora, who makes flowers bloom. The root FLOR means 'flower'.
findings: floral: covered in flowers
florist: a person who sells flowers
flora: all the plants of a place

title: The ZOO family — animal
summary: Meet Zoey, friend of every animal. The root ZOO means 'animal'.
findings: zoo: a home for many animals
zoology: the study of animals
zookeeper: a person who cares for zoo animals

title: The NOV family — new
summary: Meet Nova, who loves everything new. The root NOV means 'new'.
findings: novel: new and fresh (also a new story-book)
novice: someone new at a thing
renovate: to make new again

title: The PERSON Family — One, Many, and Beyond
summary: The Latin root PERSONA means a mask worn by an actor, and from it we get person (one human), persons/people (many humans), personal (belonging to one person), personality (their unique character), and personify (to give human traits to something).
findings: person is a word in the PERSON family.
persons is a word in the PERSON family.
people is a word in the PERSON family.
personal is a word in the PERSON family.
personality is a word in the PERSON family.
personify is a word in the PERSON family.

title: The FATHER Family — The Protector Names
summary: English 'father' comes from Old English fæder, related to Latin pater. Family words build outward: father is one parent, grandfather is two generations up, fatherly describes care like a dad gives, and fatherhood is the state of being a dad.
findings: father is a word in the FATHER family.
fatherly is a word in the FATHER family.
fatherhood is a word in the FATHER family.
grandfather is a word in the FATHER family.
dad is a word in the FATHER family.
daddy is a word in the FATHER family.

title: The MOTHER Family — Nurture Words
summary: Old English modor connects to Latin mater. The family tree: mother (one parent), grandmother (two generations up), motherly (gentle and caring), and motherhood (being a mother). Every culture has a special mother-word.
findings: mother is a word in the MOTHER family.
motherly is a word in the MOTHER family.
motherhood is a word in the MOTHER family.
grandmother is a word in the MOTHER family.
mom is a word in the MOTHER family.
mommy is a word in the MOTHER family.

title: The PARENT Family — Caregivers
summary: From Latin parens (one who brings forth). A parent protects and raises a child. Parents is the plural. Parental love is a parent's care. Parenthood is the job of being a parent. Guardian is a person who looks after someone.
findings: parent is a word in the PARENT family.
parents is a word in the PARENT family.
parental is a word in the PARENT family.
parenthood is a word in the PARENT family.
parenting is a word in the PARENT family.
guardian is a word in the PARENT family.

title: The CHILD Family — Growing Up
summary: Old English cild means a young human. Children is the irregular plural. Childhood is your growing-up years. Childlike means innocent wonder (positive). Childish means immature (negative). Baby and infant are the youngest children. Toddler is a child learning to walk.
findings: child is a word in the CHILD family.
children is a word in the CHILD family.
childhood is a word in the CHILD family.
childlike is a word in the CHILD family.
childish is a word in the CHILD family.
baby is a word in the CHILD family.

title: The FAMILY Word Web — Bonds and Belonging
summary: Latin familia means a household including servants. Family ties extend through blood and love. Families is the plural. Familiar means well-known (like family). Kin are blood relatives. A household is everyone living under one roof.
findings: family is a word in the FAMILY family.
families is a word in the FAMILY family.
familiar is a word in the FAMILY family.
familial is a word in the FAMILY family.
relative is a word in the FAMILY family.
kin is a word in the FAMILY family.

title: The BROTHER Family — Brotherhood Bonds
summary: Old English brothor, related to Latin frater. A brother is a male sibling. Brotherly means friendly and loyal (like brothers are). Brotherhood is the bond between brothers — or any close group. A sibling is any brother or sister.
findings: brother is a word in the BROTHER family.
brothers is a word in the BROTHER family.
brotherly is a word in the BROTHER family.
brotherhood is a word in the BROTHER family.
sibling is a word in the BROTHER family.
stepbrother is a word in the BROTHER family.

title: The SISTER Family — Sisterhood
summary: Old English sweostor, related to Latin soror. A sister is a female sibling. Sisterly means kind and protective. Sisterhood is the close bond between sisters. The word sibling covers both brothers and sisters.
findings: sister is a word in the SISTER family.
sisters is a word in the SISTER family.
sisterly is a word in the SISTER family.
sisterhood is a word in the SISTER family.
sibling is a word in the SISTER family.
stepsister is a word in the SISTER family.

title: The FRIEND Family — Companions
summary: Old English freond means one who loves. A friend is someone you care about. Friends are many. Friendly describes a warm nature. Friendship is the bond. Befriend means make friends. Unfriendly means cold or mean. Friendless means having no friends — a sad word.
findings: friend is a word in the FRIEND family.
friends is a word in the FRIEND family.
friendly is a word in the FRIEND family.
friendship is a word in the FRIEND family.
befriend is a word in the FRIEND family.
unfriendly is a word in the FRIEND family.

title: The NEIGHBOR Family — Near and Next-Door
summary: Old English neahgebur — 'near' + 'dweller'. A neighbor lives next to you. Neighbourhood is the area where neighbours live. Neighbourly means helpful (like a good neighbour). Neighbouring means next to something.
findings: neighbor is a word in the NEIGHBOR family.
neighbours is a word in the NEIGHBOR family.
neighbourhood is a word in the NEIGHBOR family.
neighbourly is a word in the NEIGHBOR family.
neighbouring is a word in the NEIGHBOR family.
neighborly is a word in the NEIGHBOR family.

title: The MAN Family — The Human Root
summary: Old English mann meant human (not just male). Man = one adult male, men = many. Mankind = all humans. Human comes from Latin humanus, sharing the same ancient root. Gentleman is a polite, honorable man.
findings: man is a word in the MAN family.
men is a word in the MAN family.
manly is a word in the MAN family.
manhood is a word in the MAN family.
mankind is a word in the MAN family.
gentleman is a word in the MAN family.

title: The WOMAN Family — Womanhood
summary: Old English wifman — 'wife' + 'human'. Woman = one adult female, women = many. Womanly means graceful and strong. Womanhood is being a woman. Lady is a polite word for woman. Female is the biological word.
findings: woman is a word in the WOMAN family.
women is a word in the WOMAN family.
womanly is a word in the WOMAN family.
womanhood is a word in the WOMAN family.
womankind is a word in the WOMAN family.
lady is a word in the WOMAN family.

title: The BOY and GIRL Family — Young Ones
summary: Boy and girl are the words for young humans. A boy is a male child; a girl is a female child. Boyhood and girlhood are your growing years. Boyish means like a boy (playful, rough). Girlish means like a girl (sweet, lively). These words teach us that language marks age and gender differently.
findings: boy is a word in the BOY / GIRL family.
boys is a word in the BOY / GIRL family.
boyhood is a word in the BOY / GIRL family.
boyish is a word in the BOY / GIRL family.
girl is a word in the BOY / GIRL family.
girls is a word in the BOY / GIRL family.

title: The CHAIR Family — Places to Sit
summary: Old French chaiere, from Latin cathedra (seat). A chair has a back and four legs. An armchair has armrests. A wheelchair helps people move. A bench is a long chair for many people. A stool has no back. A throne is a special chair for a king or queen.
findings: chair is a word in the CHAIR family.
chairs is a word in the CHAIR family.
armchair is a word in the CHAIR family.
wheelchair is a word in the CHAIR family.
bench is a word in the CHAIR family.
seat is a word in the CHAIR family.

title: The TABLE Family — Flat Surfaces
summary: Latin tabula means a flat board. A table is a flat top with legs. A tablecloth covers it for meals. A dining table is for eating together. A timetable is a chart of times. A tablet was once a flat writing surface.
findings: table is a word in the TABLE family.
tables is a word in the TABLE family.
tabletop is a word in the TABLE family.
tablecloth is a word in the TABLE family.
timetable is a word in the TABLE family.
tablet is a word in the TABLE family.

title: The CUP Family — Drinking Vessels
summary: Old English cuppe from Latin cupa (tub). A cup holds liquid for drinking. A teacup is a small cup for tea. A cupboard is a cabinet where cups live (board = shelf). A cupcake is a tiny cake baked in a cup-shaped mold.
findings: cup is a word in the CUP family.
cups is a word in the CUP family.
teacup is a word in the CUP family.
cupful is a word in the CUP family.
cupcake is a word in the CUP family.
cupboard is a word in the CUP family.

title: The BOTTLE Family — Liquid Containers
summary: Old French bouteille from Latin buttis (cask). A bottle holds liquids. A water bottle is for carrying water. A feeding bottle feeds a baby. Bottled means sealed in bottles. A bottlecap keeps the liquid inside.
findings: bottle is a word in the BOTTLE family.
bottles is a word in the BOTTLE family.
bottled is a word in the BOTTLE family.
bottlecap is a word in the BOTTLE family.
water bottle is a word in the BOTTLE family.
feeding bottle is a word in the BOTTLE family.

title: The BOOK Family — Written Worlds
summary: Old English boc means a written document. A book holds stories and knowledge. A booklet is a small book. A bookcase stores books. A bookmark saves your place. A notebook is for writing. A storybook tells tales. A textbook teaches.
findings: book is a word in the BOOK family.
books is a word in the BOOK family.
booklet is a word in the BOOK family.
bookcase is a word in the BOOK family.
bookmark is a word in the BOOK family.
notebook is a word in the BOOK family.

title: The DOG Family — Our Oldest Friends
summary: Old English docga. A dog is a four-legged friend. Dogs is the plural. A puppy is a baby dog. A doghouse is a tiny house for a dog. Doggy is a cute name. Hound is a hunting dog. Canine means related to dogs.
findings: dog is a word in the DOG family.
dogs is a word in the DOG family.
doghouse is a word in the DOG family.
doggy is a word in the DOG family.
puppy is a word in the DOG family.
hound is a word in the DOG family.

title: The CAT Family — Independent Companions
summary: Old English catt from Latin catta. A cat is a small furry pet. A kitten is a baby cat. Catlike means graceful and quiet. Feline means related to cats. Catnip is a plant cats love. Caterwaul is a loud cat cry.
findings: cat is a word in the CAT family.
cats is a word in the CAT family.
catnip is a word in the CAT family.
catlike is a word in the CAT family.
caterwaul is a word in the CAT family.
kitten is a word in the CAT family.

title: The FOOD Word Family — What We Eat
summary: Old English foda. Food gives us energy. To feed means give food. An apple is a round fruit. A banana is a yellow fruit. Bread is made from flour. Pizza comes from Italy. Carrots are orange vegetables. Cake is a sweet treat for special days.
findings: food is a word in the FOOD / EAT family.
foods is a word in the FOOD / EAT family.
feed is a word in the FOOD / EAT family.
foodie is a word in the FOOD / EAT family.
apple is a word in the FOOD / EAT family.
banana is a word in the FOOD / EAT family.

title: The VEHICLE Family — Ways We Travel
summary: Latin vehiculum means a conveyance. A car has an engine and four wheels. A bicycle (bi=two, cycle=circle) has two wheels. A bus carries many people. A train runs on tracks. A boat sails on water. Every vehicle moves us from here to there.
findings: car is a word in the VEHICLE family.
cars is a word in the VEHICLE family.
bicycle is a word in the VEHICLE family.
bike is a word in the VEHICLE family.
bus is a word in the VEHICLE family.
train is a word in the VEHICLE family.

title: The HOME Family — Where We Live
summary: Old English ham means dwelling. A home is where you live. A house is the building. Homely means cosy and welcoming. Homework is schoolwork done at home. A bed is for sleeping. A door lets you in. A window lets in light. A roof keeps rain out. A kitchen is for cooking.
findings: home is a word in the HOME family.
house is a word in the HOME family.
homes is a word in the HOME family.
homely is a word in the HOME family.
homework is a word in the HOME family.
homeless is a word in the HOME family.

summary: The letters 'ch' represent a single sound. This is called a digraph — two letters making one sound.
